blocks:
Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ ٱتَّقِ ٱللَّهَ وَلَا تُطِعِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا (١)
O Prophet! Be wary of Allah and do not obey the faithless and the hypocrites. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (1)
وَٱتَّبِعْ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا (٢)
Follow that which is revealed to you from your Lord. Indeed Allah is well aware of what you do. (2)
وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ وَكِيلًا (٣)
And put your trust in Allah; Allah suffices as trustee. (3)
مَّا جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ لِرَجُلٍ مِّن قَلْبَيْنِ فِى جَوْفِهِۦ ۚ وَمَا جَعَلَ أَزْوَٰجَكُمُ ٱلَّـٰٓـِٔى تُظَـٰهِرُونَ مِنْهُنَّ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ ۚ وَمَا جَعَلَ أَدْعِيَآءَكُمْ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ قَوْلُكُم بِأَفْوَٰهِكُمْ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَقُولُ ٱلْحَقَّ وَهُوَ يَهْدِى ٱلسَّبِيلَ (٤)
Allah has not put two hearts within any man, nor has He made your wives whom you repudiate by zihar your mothers, nor has he made your adopted sons your [actual] sons. These are mere utterances of your mouths. But Allah speaks the truth and He guides to the [right] way. (4)
ٱدْعُوهُمْ لِـَٔابَآئِهِمْ هُوَ أَقْسَطُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ تَعْلَمُوٓا۟ ءَابَآءَهُمْ فَإِخْوَٰنُكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَمَوَٰلِيكُمْ ۚ وَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ فِيمَآ أَخْطَأْتُم بِهِۦ وَلَـٰكِن مَّا تَعَمَّدَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (٥)
Call them after their fathers. That is more just with Allah. If you do not know their fathers, then they are your brethren in the faith and your kinsmen. Excepting what your hearts may intend deliberately, there will be no sin upon you for any mistake that you may make therein. And Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (5)
ٱلنَّبِىُّ أَوْلَىٰ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۖ وَأَزْوَٰجُهُۥٓ أُمَّهَـٰتُهُمْ ۗ وَأُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْحَامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِبَعْضٍ فِى كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُهَـٰجِرِينَ إِلَّآ أَن تَفْعَلُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَوْلِيَآئِكُم مَّعْرُوفًا ۚ كَانَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مَسْطُورًا (٦)
The Prophet is closer to the faithful than their own souls, and his wives are their mothers. The blood relatives are more entitled to inherit from one another in the Book of Allah than the [other] faithful and Emigrants, barring any favour you may do your comrades. This has been written in the Book. (6)
وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِنَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ مِيثَـٰقَهُمْ وَمِنكَ وَمِن نُّوحٍ وَإِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ ۖ وَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُم مِّيثَـٰقًا غَلِيظًا (٧)
[Recall] when We took a pledge from the prophets, and from you and from Noah and Abraham and Moses and Jesus son of Mary, and We took from them a solemn pledge, (7)
لِّيَسْـَٔلَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ عَن صِدْقِهِمْ ۚ وَأَعَدَّ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (٨)
so that He may question the truthful concerning their truthfulness. And He has prepared for the faithless a painful punishment. (8)

(Commentary)

The Surah contains various fragments of knowledge (المعارف), rulings (الأحكام), stories (القصص), lessons (العبر), and exhortations (المواعظ). It includes the account of the Battle of the Trench (غزوة الخندق) and an allusion to the story of the Banu Qurayzah from among the Jews. The context of its verses testifies that it was among those revealed in Medina.

His saying, the Exalted: «يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ وَلَا تُطِعِ الْكَافِرِينَ وَالْمُنَافِقِينَ ۗ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا» O Prophet! Be God-conscious, and do not obey the faithless and the hypocrites. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (33:1). This is a command to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to maintain godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah's presence and avoiding His displeasure — and it serves as a preamble to the prohibition that follows: «وَلَا تُطِعِ الْكَافِرِينَ وَالْمُنَافِقِينَ» and do not obey the faithless and the hypocrites.

The context of the prohibition—combining the disbelievers (الكافرين) and the hypocrites (المنافقين) and forbidding obedience to them—reveals that the disbelievers were asking him for something that Allah, the Glorified, did not approve of, while the hypocrites were supporting them in their request and being persistent. It was a matter concerning which Allah, the Glorified, by His knowledge and wisdom, had already decreed otherwise, and divine revelation (الوحي الإلهي) had descended to the contrary. It was a grave matter where the alignment of external factors might have favored the opposite outcome unless Allah willed otherwise. Thus, He warned the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) against granting their request and commanded him to follow what Allah revealed to him and to rely upon Him.

This supports what has been narrated regarding the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول): that a number of the chieftains of Quraysh, after the Battle of Uhud, entered Medina under a guarantee of safety from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and asked him to leave them and their idols alone, in exchange for which they would leave him and his God alone. Consequently, these verses were revealed, and the Prophet did not grant them that request, as will be discussed in the following traditional report.

From what has preceded, the reason for concluding the verse with His saying: «إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا» Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-wise becomes clear, as does the reason for following this verse with the two verses after it.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَاتَّبِعْ مَا يُوحَىٰ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا» Follow what is revealed to you from your Lord; indeed Allah is well-aware of what you do. (33:2).

The verse is general in its own right, but because it occurs within the context of the prohibition, it commands the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to follow what has been sent down through revelation (الوحي) regarding what the disbelievers and hypocrites were asking of him. His "following" it means implementing it in action, as evidenced by His saying: «إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا» indeed Allah is well-aware of what you do.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِاللَّهِ وَكِيلًا» And put your trust in Allah; Allah suffices as a trustee. (33:3). This verse is like the preceding one in that it is general in its own right. However, because it occurs in the context of the previous prohibition, it indicates a command to place trust (التوكل) — the act of relying solely upon Allah while delegating one's affairs to Him — in Allah regarding what the revelation commands him. It suggests that the matter is difficult to achieve when considering apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرية), such that the heart cannot remain safe from the onset of fear and agitation except through trust in Allah, the Glorified; for He is the only cause (السبب) that no opposing cause can overcome.

His saying, the Exalted: «مَّا جَعَلَ اللَّهُ لِرَجُلٍ مِّن قَلْبَيْنِ فِي جَوْفِهِ» Allah has not put two hearts within any man. (33:4). This is an allusion (كناية) to the impossibility of combining two mutually exclusive beliefs (المتنافيين في الاعتقاد). A single heart—meaning a single soul (النفس الواحدة)—cannot accommodate two incompatible beliefs or two contradictory opinions. If there were two incompatible things, they would belong to two hearts, but Allah has not placed two hearts within any man's breast. Thus, a single man cannot believe in two incompatible things or affirm two contradictions. His saying: «فِي جَوْفِهِ» within his breast (literally: in his hollow) serves to emphasize the point, similar to His saying: «وَلَٰكِن تَعْمَى الْقُلُوبُ الَّتِي فِي الصُّدُورِ» but it is the hearts in the breasts that turn blind. (22:46).

It has been said that the sentence is a preamble and preparation, acting as a justification (التعليل) for what follows regarding the annulment of the practice of zihar (الظهار) and adoption (التبني). In zihar, a man would treat his wife as being in the position of his mother, and in adoption and calling someone a son, he would treat another's child as his own. Combining the status of wife and mother, as well as combining the sonship of another with one's own sonship, is a combination of incompatible things. They cannot coexist except in two hearts, and Allah has not placed two hearts within any man.

It is not improbable that the sentence serves as a justification for His previous saying: «لَا تُطِعِ الْكَافِرِينَ وَالْمُنَافِقِينَ» do not obey the faithless and the hypocrites and «وَاتَّبِعْ مَا يُوحَىٰ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ» Follow what is revealed to you from your Lord. This is because obedience to Allah and His guardianship (ولايته), and obedience to the disbelievers and hypocrites and their guardianship, are incompatible and distinct, like monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and polytheism (الشرك). They cannot coexist in a single heart, and Allah has not placed two hearts within any man.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَمَا جَعَلَ أَزْواجَكُمُ اللَّائِي تُظاهِرُونَ مِنْهُنَّ أُمَّهاتِكُمْ» nor has He made your wives whom you repudiate by zihar your mothers. (33:4). In the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية), a man would say to his wife, "You are to me like the back of my mother," or "Your back is to me like the back of my mother." He would liken her back to his mother's back, and this was called zihar (ظهارا) and was considered a divorce (طلاقا) for her. Islam annulled this.

The meaning of the verse is that Allah did not make your wives, from whom you separate by saying "Your back is to me like the back of my mother," into mothers for you. Since He did not make them so, there is no effect to this statement; the "making" (الجعل) here is legislative (تشريعي).

His saying, the Exalted: «وَمَا جَعَلَ أَدْعِيَاءَكُمْ أَبْنَاءَكُمْ» nor has He made your adopted sons your [real] sons. (33:4). The adopted sons (الأدعياء) is the plural of da'iyy (دعي), which refers to one taken as a son and called a son. Calling others sons and adoption (التبني) were prevalent among them in the Age of Ignorance, as well as among the advanced nations of that time like Rome and Persia. They used to apply the rulings of biological sons (الولد الصلبي) to the adopted son, such as inheritance (التوارث), the prohibition of marriage, and other matters. Islam annulled this.

The meaning of the verse is that Allah did not make those whom you claim for yourselves as sons into your actual sons, such that the rulings applicable to biological sons would apply to them.

His saying, the Exalted: «ذَٰلِكُمْ قَوْلُكُم بِأَفْوَاهِكُمْ ۖ وَاللَّهُ يَقُولُ الْحَقَّ وَهُوَ يَهْدِي السَّبِيلَ» These are mere words of your mouths; but Allah speaks the truth and He guides to the [right] way. (33:4). The demonstration (الإشارة) in His saying: «ذَٰلِكُمْ» These refers to what preceded regarding zihar and adoption, or to adoption alone, which is more apparent and is supported by the fact that the following verse is restricted to the ruling on adoption only.

His saying: «قَوْلُكُم بِأَفْوَاهِكُمْ» mere words of your mouths means that the attribution of the adopted son to yourselves is nothing but a statement you utter with your mouths, having no reality beyond that. It is an allusion to the absence of any actual effect, as in His saying: «كَلَّا ۚ إِنَّهَا كَلِمَةٌ هُوَ قَائِلُهَا» No! It is just a word he utters. (23:100).

His saying: «وَاللَّهُ يَقُولُ الْحَقَّ وَهُوَ يَهْدِي السَّبِيلَ» but Allah speaks the truth and He guides to the [right] way. The meaning of His speech being "the truth" (الحق) is that if He informs of something, reality corresponds to what He has informed; and if He initiates a ruling, its effects follow, and it corresponds to the actual benefit (المصلحة الواقعية).

The meaning of His guiding to the way (هدايته السبيل) is that He directs the one He guides to the path of truth (سبيل الحق) which contains goodness and felicity. In these two sentences, there is an intimation to leave your own words and take His word.

His saying, the Exalted: «ادْعُوهُمْ لِآبَائِهِمْ هُوَ أَقْسَطُ عِندَ اللَّهِ» Call them by [the names of] their fathers; that is more just with Allah. (33:5) to the end of the verse. The preposition lam in «لِآبَائِهِمْ» denotes exclusivity (الاختصاص); meaning, call them while they are exclusively attributed to their fathers. His saying: «هُوَ أَقْسَطُ عِندَ اللَّهِ» that is more just with Allah, the pronoun refers to the verbal noun (المصدر) understood from His saying: «ادْعُوهُمْ» Call them, similar to His saying: «اعْدِلُوا هُوَ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ» Be just; that is nearer to godwariness. (5:8). More just (أقسط) is a superlative form of qist (القسط), meaning justice (العدل).

The meaning is: Attribute them to their fathers—when you call them—because calling them by their fathers is more just in the sight of Allah.

His saying: «فَإِن لَّمْ تَعْلَمُوا آبَاءَهُمْ فَإِخْوَانُكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ وَمَوَالِيكُمْ» But if you do not know their fathers, then they are your brethren in faith and your wards. (33:5). What is meant by not knowing their fathers is not knowing their specific identities. The wards (الموالي) are the protégés or allies (الأولياء). The meaning is: If you do not know their fathers, do not attribute them to anyone other than their fathers; rather, call them by brotherhood and religious guardianship (الولاية الدينية).

His saying: «وَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ فِيمَا أَخْطَأْتُم بِهِ وَلَٰكِن مَّا تَعَمَّدَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ» There is no sin upon you in what you may mistake therein, but [only in] what your hearts intend. (33:5). That is, there is no sin upon you for what you did by mistake due to oversight or forgetfulness, such as calling them by other than their fathers; but that which your hearts intended is a sin—or rather, the intentionality of your hearts in that matter contains the sin.

His saying: «وَكَانَ اللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا» And Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (33:5) refers to what was done by mistake.

His saying, the Exalted: «النَّبِيُّ أَوْلَىٰ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۖ وَأَزْوَاجُهُ أُمَّهَاتُهُمْ» The Prophet is closer to the faithful than their own souls, and his wives are their mothers. (33:6). The "souls of the believers" are the believers themselves. Thus, the meaning of the Prophet being "closer" (أولى) to them than their own souls is that he has more right over them than they have over themselves. The meaning of this "priority" (الأولوية) is the preponderance of one side when a choice must be made between it and that which it has priority over. The result is that whatever a believer deems for himself in terms of protection, care, love, honor, responding to a call, and executing a will, the Prophet has more right to that than the believer's own self. If a matter were to revolve between the Prophet and one's own self in any of these things, the side of the Prophet would be more preponderant than the side of one's own self.

Therefore, if any danger were to be directed toward the person of the Prophet, the believer must shield him with his own self and sacrifice his life for him. The Prophet should be more beloved to him than his own self and more honored by him than his own self. If his soul calls him to something and the Prophet calls him to the opposite, or if his soul desires something from him and the Prophet desires the opposite, it is mandatory to respond to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), obey him, and give him precedence over oneself.

Likewise, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) has more right over them in matters pertaining to worldly or religious affairs; all of this is due to the absolute nature (الإطلاق) of His saying: «النَّبِيُّ أَوْلَىٰ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ» The Prophet is closer to the faithful than their own souls.

From here, the weakness of what has been said becomes apparent: that the meaning is he has more right over them in the call (الدعوة), such that if he calls them to something and their souls call them to the opposite, they must obey him and disobey their souls. Thus, the verse would be in the sense of His saying: «وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ» and obey the Apostle (4:59) and His saying: «وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطَاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ» We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave (4:64) and similar verses. This is refuted by the absolute nature of the phrasing.

Similarly, what has been said—that the meaning is his judgment among them is more effective than the judgment of some of them over others, as in His saying: «فَسَلِّمُوا عَلَىٰ أَنفُسِكُمْ» greet your own souls (24:61)—leads to the conclusion that his guardianship (ولايته) over the believers is above the guardianship of some of them over others, as indicated by His saying: «الْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَالْمُؤْمِنَاتُ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَاءُ بَعْضٍ» The faithful, men and women, are allies of one another (9:71).

However, the context does not support this interpretation.

His saying: «وَأَزْوَاجُهُ أُمَّهَاتُهُمْ» and his wives are their mothers is a legislative making (جعل تشريعي); meaning that they are in the position of their mothers regarding the obligation of honoring them and the prohibition of marrying them after the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), as will be explicitly stated in His saying: «وَلَا أَن تَنكِحُوا أَزْوَاجَهُ مِن بَعْدِهِ أَبَدًا» nor that you should ever marry his wives after him. (33:53).

Thus, the comparison (التنزيل) is only in some effects of motherhood, not in all effects, such as inheritance between them and the believers, or looking at their faces as one looks at mothers, or the prohibition of their daughters to the believers—as if they had become sisters to them—or their fathers and mothers becoming grandfathers and grandmothers, and their brothers and sisters becoming maternal uncles and aunts to the believers.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَأُولُو الْأَرْحَامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُهَاجِرِينَ» Blood relatives are closer to one another in the Book of Allah than the [other] faithful and the Emigrants (33:6) etc. The wombs (الأرحام) is the plural of rahm (رحم), which is the organ that carries the sperm until it becomes a fetus and is born. Since blood kinship (القرابة النسبية) necessarily traces back to a single womb, kinship is expressed by the term "womb," and those possessed of kinship are called "blood relatives" (أولي الأرحام).

What is meant by blood relatives being "closer to one another" is priority in inheritance (التوارث). His saying: «فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ» in the Book of Allah refers to the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), the Quran, or the Surah. His saying: «مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُهَاجِرِينَ» than the [other] faithful and the Emigrants is the object of comparison. What is meant by "the faithful" here is those other than the Emigrants. The meaning is: Those possessed of kinship have more right to one another than the Emigrants and the rest of the believers who used to inherit through religious brotherhood (المؤاخاة الدينية). This priority is in the Book of Allah. It is also possible that His saying: «مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُهَاجِرِينَ» is an explanation (بيان) for His saying: «وَأُولُو الْأَرْحَامِ».

The verse abrogates (ناسخة) what existed in the early days of Islam regarding inheritance through migration (الهجرة) and religious alliance (الموالاة في الدين).

His saying: «إِلَّا أَن تَفْعَلُوا إِلَىٰ أَوْلِيَائِكُم مَّعْرُوفًا» unless you do a kindness to your friends. (33:6). The exception is disconnected (منقطع). What is meant by "doing kindness to friends" is making a bequest (الوصية) to them from the estate, which has been legally limited to one-third of the wealth or less. His saying: «كَانَ ذَٰلِكَ فِي الْكِتَابِ مَسْطُورًا» This has been written in the Book. (33:6) means the ruling of doing kindness through a bequest is inscribed in the Preserved Tablet, the Quran, or the Surah.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ مِيثَاقَهُمْ وَمِنكَ وَمِن نُّوحٍ وَإِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ ۖ وَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُم مِّيثَاقًا غَلِيظًا» [Remember] when We took a pledge from the prophets, and from you, and from Noah and Abraham and Moses and Jesus son of Mary, and We took from them a solemn pledge. (33:7). The attribution of the pledge (الميثاق) to the pronoun of the prophets is evidence that what is meant is a pledge specific to them, just as mentioning them with the attribute of prophethood suggests this. Thus, the pledge taken from the prophets is a specific pledge in their capacity as prophets, and it is different from the pledge taken from all of humanity, which He refers to in His saying: «وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِن بَنِي آدَمَ مِن ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلَىٰ أَنفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ ۖ قَالُوا بَلَىٰ» When your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants and made them bear witness over themselves, [saying to them,] ‘Am I not your Lord?’ They said, ‘Yes indeed!’ (7:172).

The taking of the pledge from the prophets is mentioned in another place, which is His saying: «وَإِذْ أَخَذَ اللَّهُ مِيثَاقَ النَّبِيِّينَ لَمَا آتَيْتُكُم مِّن كِتَابٍ وَحِكْمَةٍ ثُمَّ جَاءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مُّصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَكُمْ لَتُؤْمِنُنَّ بِهِ وَلَتَنْصُرُنَّهُ ۚ قَالَ أَأَقْرَرْتُمْ وَأَخَذْتُمْ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكُمْ إِصْرِي ۖ قَالُوا أَقْرَرْنَا» When Allah took a pledge from the prophets, [saying,] ‘Whatever I give you of the Book and wisdom, and then an apostle comes to you confirming what is with you, you shall surely believe in him and help him.’ He said, ‘Do you agree and accept My pledge in this matter?’ They said, ‘We agree.’ (3:81).

Although the verse under discussion does not explain what the pledge taken from them was—even if it contains an allusion that it is a matter related to prophethood—it can be inferred from the verse in Al-Imran that the pledge was taken regarding the unity of the word in religion and the absence of disagreement therein, as in His saying: «إِنَّ هَٰذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُونِ» Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord. So worship Me. (21:92) and His saying: «شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ الدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِ نُوحًا وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ ۖ أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ» He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus, [declaring,] ‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it.’ (42:13).

He mentioned the prophets with a general term that includes all of them, then named five of them by their names through conjunction (العطف), saying: «وَمِنكَ وَمِن نُّوحٍ وَإِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ» and from you, and from Noah and Abraham and Moses and Jesus son of Mary. The meaning of the conjunction is to distinguish them from among the rest and to specify them for mention, as if it were said: "And when We took the pledge from you five and from the rest of the prophets."

He did not specify them for mention in this manner except due to the greatness of their status and the loftiness of their position; for they are the possessors of resolve (أولوا عزم) and the masters of divine laws (شرائع) and books. He listed them according to the order of their time: Noah, then Abraham, then Moses, then Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him). However, He gave precedence to the mention of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who is the last of them in time, due to his excellence, nobility, and precedence over all.

His saying: «وَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُم مِّيثَاقًا غَلِيظًا» and We took from them a solemn pledge is an emphasis and intensification of the pledge, similar to His saying: «وَلَمَّا جَاءَ أَمْرُنَا نَجَّيْنَا هُودًا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَنَجَّيْنَاهُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ غَلِيظٍ» When Our edict came, We delivered Hud and those who had faith with him by a mercy from Us, and We delivered them from a harsh punishment. (11:58).

His saying, the Exalted: «لِّيَسْأَلَ الصَّادِقِينَ عَن صِدْقِهِمْ ۚ وَأَعَدَّ لِلْكَافِرِينَ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا» So that He may question the truthful concerning their truthfulness, and He has prepared a painful punishment for the faithless. (33:8). The preposition lam in «لِّيَسْأَلَ» denotes causality (التعليل) or purpose (الغاية), and it is connected to a deleted verb indicated by His saying: «وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا» when We took, and His saying: «وَأَعَدَّ» and He has prepared is conjoined to that deleted verb. The estimation is: He did that—meaning the taking of the pledge—so that the questioning of the truthful concerning their truthfulness might be established for Him, and He prepared for the disbelievers a painful punishment.

He did not say: "and so that He may prepare for the disbelievers a punishment," to intimate that their punishment is not among the final causes (العلل الغائية) for taking the pledge; rather, the deficiency is from their side and the breach is from them.

As for the questioning of the truthful concerning their truthfulness, it has been said: What is meant by "the truthful" (الصادقين) is the prophets, and questioning them about their truthfulness is questioning them on the Day of Resurrection about what their nations brought—as if taken from His saying: «يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ اللَّهُ الرُّسُلَ فَيَقُولُ مَاذَا أُجِبْتُمْ» The day Allah will gather the apostles and say, ‘What was the response you received?’ (5:109).

It has also been said: What is meant is questioning the truthful regarding the monotheism of Allah, His justice, and the divine laws about their truthfulness—meaning about what they used to say concerning it. It has been said: What is meant is questioning the truthful in their words about their truthfulness in their actions. It has been said: What is meant is questioning the truthful about what they intended by their truthfulness—was it for the sake of Allah or otherwise? Among other views, which are as you see.

Contemplation of what His saying: «لِّيَسْأَلَ الصَّادِقِينَ عَن صِدْقِهِمْ» So that He may question the truthful concerning their truthfulness provides, guides to something other than what they mentioned. There is a difference between saying: "I asked the wealthy man about his wealth" or "I asked the scholar about his knowledge," and saying: "I asked Zayd about his property" or "about his knowledge." What is immediately understood from the first two is that I demanded he manifest his wealth and manifest his knowledge; whereas from the latter two, it is understood that I demanded he inform me whether he has property or whether he has knowledge, or that he describe to me what property or knowledge he has.

Based on this, the meaning of questioning the truthful concerning their truthfulness is demanding that they manifest what is in their inner being of truthfulness at the level of word and deed, which is their righteous deeds (عملهم الصالح) in this world. Thus, what is meant by questioning the truthful concerning their truthfulness is directing the religious obligation (التكليف) to them according to the pledge, so that their truthfulness, which is latent in their souls, may manifest from them. This occurs in this world, not in the hereafter. Thus, the taking of the pledge occurred in another realm of existence (نشأة أخرى) before this world, as indicated by the verses of the Progeny (آيات الذر): «وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِن بَنِي آدَمَ مِن ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلَىٰ أَنفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ ۖ قَالُوا بَلَىٰ» When your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants and made them bear witness over themselves, [saying to them,] ‘Am I not your Lord?’ They said, ‘Yes indeed!’ (7:172).

In short, the two verses are among those informing of the World of the Progeny (عالم الذر) — the primordial state of existence where the souls of all human beings were brought forth and pledged their recognition of Allah's Lordship — in which the pledge was taken. They mention that the taking of the pledge from the prophets (peace be upon them) and the arrangement of their status and actions in this world based on that, is within the context of the arrangement of the truthfulness of every truthful person based on the pledge taken from him.

Due to this generalization, He mentioned the end of the disbelievers' affair, even though they are not of the same category as the prophets and the discussion was about the pledge taken from them. It is as if it were said: "We took a solemn pledge from the prophets that their word should agree on one religion which they would convey, so that He may question the truthful and demand from them religious obligation and guidance to manifest their truthfulness in belief and action. They did so, and thus reward was decreed for them, and He prepared for the disbelievers a painful punishment."

From here, the reason for the shift (الالتفات) from the first-person plural to the third-person singular in His saying: «لِّيَسْأَلَ الصَّادِقِينَ» So that He may question the truthful etc., becomes clear. This is because the pledge is for His worship alone without any partner, even if its taking from Him was through the mediation of the angels—which justifies the use of "We took" (أَخَذْنَا). Thus, the one who demands the truthfulness of the truthful and the one who prepares the punishment for the disbelievers is, in reality, He alone, so that He alone may be worshipped. Reflect upon this.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: «يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ» O Prophet! Be God-conscious—the verses were revealed concerning Abu Sufyan ibn Harb, Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl, and Abu al-A'war al-Sulami. They came to Medina and stayed with Abdullah ibn Ubayy—after the Battle of Uhud under a guarantee of safety from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—to speak with him. They stood up, and with them stood Abdullah ibn Ubayy, Abdullah ibn Sa'id ibn Abi Sarh, and Tu'mah ibn Ubayriq. They entered upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: "O Muhammad, abandon the mention of our goddesses al-Lat, al-Uzza, and Manat, and say: 'Indeed they have intercession for those who worship them,' and we will leave you and your Lord." This was difficult for the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Umar ibn al-Khattab said: "Give us permission, O Messenger of Allah, to kill them." He replied: "I have given them a guarantee of safety." He then ordered that they be removed from Medina, and the verse was revealed: «وَلَا تُطِعِ الْكَافِرِينَ» and do not obey the faithless—referring to the people of Mecca: Abu Sufyan, Abu al-A'war, and Ikrimah—«وَالْمُنَافِقِينَ» and the hypocrites—referring to Ibn Ubayy, Ibn Sa'id, and Tu'mah.

I say: The summary of the story is also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Jarir from Ibn Abbas. Other occasions for the revelation of the verses have been narrated, but they are extraneous and do not suit the context of the verses, so we have omitted them.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: «وَمَا جَعَلَ أَدْعِيَاءَكُمْ أَبْنَاءَكُمْ» nor has He made your adopted sons your [real] sons: My father narrated to me from Ibn Abi Umayr from Jamil from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The reason for that was that when the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) married Khadijah bint Khuwaylid, he went out to the market of Ukaz for trade and saw Zayd being sold. He saw him as a clever and robust boy and bought him. When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was commissioned as a prophet, he called him to Islam, and he accepted. He used to be called Zayd, the ward of Muhammad.

When the news of his son Zayd reached Harithah ibn Shurahil al-Kalbi, he came to Mecca—and he was an honorable man. He came to Abu Talib and said: 'O Abu Talib, my son was taken captive, and I have heard that he is with your nephew. Ask him either to sell him, or to accept a ransom for him, or to manumit him.'

Abu Talib spoke to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the Messenger of Allah said: 'He is free; let him go wherever he wishes.' Harithah stood up and took Zayd by the hand and said to him: 'O my son, join your honor and your lineage.' Zayd replied: 'I shall not leave the Messenger of Allah.' His father said to him: 'Will you leave your honor and your lineage and be a slave to Quraysh?' Zayd said: 'I shall not leave the Messenger of Allah as long as I live.' His father became angry and said: 'O people of Quraysh, bear witness that I have disowned him and he is not my son.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) then said: 'Bear witness that Zayd is my son; I shall inherit from him and he shall inherit from me.' Thus, Zayd used to be called the son of Muhammad, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) loved him and named him Zayd al-Hubb (the Beloved).

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) migrated to Medina, he married him to Zaynab bint Jahsh. One day, Zayd was delayed, so the Messenger of Allah came to his house to ask about him. He found Zaynab sitting in the middle of her room preparing her perfume with a stone pestle. The Messenger of Allah pushed the door and looked at her—and she was beautiful and fair—and said: 'Glory be to Allah, the Lord of Light, and blessed be Allah, the best of creators.' Then the Messenger of Allah returned to his house, and Zaynab occupied a remarkable place in his heart.

Zayd came to his house, and Zaynab informed him of what the Messenger of Allah had said. Zayd said to her: 'Would you like me to divorce you so that the Messenger of Allah may marry you?' She replied: 'I fear that you will divorce me and the Messenger of Allah will not marry me.' Zayd came to the Messenger of Allah and said: 'May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, O Messenger of Allah, Zaynab informed me of such and such. Would you like me to divorce her so that you may marry her?' The Messenger of Allah said to him: 'No, go and be God-conscious and keep your wife.' Then Allah recounted this, saying: «أَمْسِكْ عَلَيْكَ زَوْجَكَ وَاتَّقِ اللَّهَ وَتُخْفِي فِي نَفْسِكَ مَا اللَّهُ مُبْدِيهِ وَتَخْشَى النَّاسَ وَاللَّهُ أَحَقُّ أَن تَخْشَاهُ ۖ فَلَمَّا قَضَىٰ زَيْدٌ مِّنْهَا وَطَرًا زَوَّجْنَاكَهَا» Keep your wife for yourself and be God-conscious of Allah,’ and you were hiding in your heart what Allah was to expose, and you feared the people, though Allah is worthier that you should fear Him. So when Zayd had given up all desire for her, We gave her to you in marriage—until His saying—«وَكَانَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ مَفْعُولًا» and Allah’s command is bound to be fulfilled. (33:37). Thus, Allah married him to her from above His Throne.

The hypocrites said: 'He forbids us from the wives of our sons, yet he marries the wife of his son Zayd.' So Allah revealed regarding this: «وَمَا جَعَلَ أَدْعِيَاءَكُمْ أَبْنَاءَكُمْ» nor has He made your adopted sons your [real] sons—until His saying—«يَهْدِي السَّبِيلَ» He guides to the [right] way."

I say: Something similar to this, with some differences, is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn Abbas.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, Abu Dawud, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Jabir from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he used to say: أنا أولى بكل مؤمن من نفسه ـ فأيما رجل مات وترك دينا فالي ، ومن ترك مالا فهو لورثته — "I am closer to every believer than his own soul. Whosoever dies and leaves a debt, it is upon me; and whosoever leaves wealth, it is for his heirs."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning from both Shi'i and Sunni paths.

In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, and al-Nasa'i narrated from Buraydah, who said: "I went on a campaign with Ali to Yemen and saw some coldness from him. When I came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), I mentioned Ali and spoke disparagingly of him. I saw the face of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) change, and he said: 'O Buraydah, am I not closer to the believers than their own souls?' I said: 'Yes, O Messenger of Allah.' He said: من كنت مولاه فعلي مولاه — 'Whomsoever I am his master, Ali is his master.'"

In al-Ihtijaj, from Abdullah ibn Ja'far ibn Abi Talib in a long tradition, he said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying: 'I am closer to the believers than their own souls. Whomsoever I am closer to than his own soul, then you are closer to him than his own soul'—and Ali was before him in the house."

I say: It is narrated in al-Kafi with its chain from Ja'far from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). The traditions with this meaning from the paths of both groups are beyond counting.

In al-Kafi, with its chain from Hannan, who said: "I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'What is there for the wards (الموالي)?' He replied: 'They have nothing of the inheritance except what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: «إِلَّا أَن تَفْعَلُوا إِلَىٰ أَوْلِيَائِكُم مَّعْرُوفًا» unless you do a kindness to your friends.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "It was said: 'O Messenger of Allah, when was your pledge taken?' He replied: وآدم بين الروح والجسد — 'While Adam was between the spirit and the body.'"

I say: This is narrated with its exact wording through various paths from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Its meaning is that the pledge was taken in a realm of existence other than this one and prior to it.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ جَآءَتْكُمْ جُنُودٌ فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا وَجُنُودًا لَّمْ تَرَوْهَا ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرًا (٩)
O you who have faith! Remember Allah’s blessing upon you when the hosts came at you, and We sent against them a gale and hosts whom you did not see, and Allah sees best what you do. (9)
إِذْ جَآءُوكُم مِّن فَوْقِكُمْ وَمِنْ أَسْفَلَ مِنكُمْ وَإِذْ زَاغَتِ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ وَبَلَغَتِ ٱلْقُلُوبُ ٱلْحَنَاجِرَ وَتَظُنُّونَ بِٱللَّهِ ٱلظُّنُونَا۠ (١٠)
When they came at you from above and below you, and when the eyes rolled [with fear] and the hearts leapt to the throats, and you entertained misgivings about Allah, (10)
هُنَالِكَ ٱبْتُلِىَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَزُلْزِلُوا۟ زِلْزَالًا شَدِيدًا (١١)
it was there that the faithful were tested and jolted with a severe agitation. (11)
وَإِذْ يَقُولُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ مَّا وَعَدَنَا ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥٓ إِلَّا غُرُورًا (١٢)
When the hypocrites, as well as those in whose hearts is a sickness, were saying, ‘Allah and His Apostle did not promise us [anything] except delusion.’ (12)
وَإِذْ قَالَت طَّآئِفَةٌ مِّنْهُمْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ يَثْرِبَ لَا مُقَامَ لَكُمْ فَٱرْجِعُوا۟ ۚ وَيَسْتَـْٔذِنُ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمُ ٱلنَّبِىَّ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّ بُيُوتَنَا عَوْرَةٌ وَمَا هِىَ بِعَوْرَةٍ ۖ إِن يُرِيدُونَ إِلَّا فِرَارًا (١٣)
And when a group of them said, ‘O people of Yathrib! [This is] not a place for you, so go back!’ A group of them sought the Prophet’s permission [to leave the scene of battle], saying, ‘Our homes lie exposed [to the enemy],’ although they were not exposed. They only sought to flee. (13)
وَلَوْ دُخِلَتْ عَلَيْهِم مِّنْ أَقْطَارِهَا ثُمَّ سُئِلُوا۟ ٱلْفِتْنَةَ لَـَٔاتَوْهَا وَمَا تَلَبَّثُوا۟ بِهَآ إِلَّا يَسِيرًا (١٤)
Had they been invaded from its flanks and had they been asked to apostatize, they would have done so with only a mild hesitation, (14)
وَلَقَدْ كَانُوا۟ عَـٰهَدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مِن قَبْلُ لَا يُوَلُّونَ ٱلْأَدْبَـٰرَ ۚ وَكَانَ عَهْدُ ٱللَّهِ مَسْـُٔولًا (١٥)
though they had already pledged to Allah before that they would not turn their backs [to flee], and pledges given to Allah are accountable. (15)
قُل لَّن يَنفَعَكُمُ ٱلْفِرَارُ إِن فَرَرْتُم مِّنَ ٱلْمَوْتِ أَوِ ٱلْقَتْلِ وَإِذًا لَّا تُمَتَّعُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (١٦)
Say, ‘Flight will not avail you, if you flee from death, or from being killed, and then you will be let to enjoy only for a little while.’ (16)
قُلْ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَعْصِمُكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ إِنْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ سُوٓءًا أَوْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ رَحْمَةً ۚ وَلَا يَجِدُونَ لَهُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (١٧)
Say, ‘Who is it that can protect you from Allah if He desires to cause you harm or desires to grant you His mercy?’ Besides Allah they will not find for themselves any protector or helper. (17)
۞ قَدْ يَعْلَمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُعَوِّقِينَ مِنكُمْ وَٱلْقَآئِلِينَ لِإِخْوَٰنِهِمْ هَلُمَّ إِلَيْنَا ۖ وَلَا يَأْتُونَ ٱلْبَأْسَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (١٨)
Allah knows those of you who discourage others, and those who say to their brethren, ‘Come to us!’ and take little part in the battle, (18)
أَشِحَّةً عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ ٱلْخَوْفُ رَأَيْتَهُمْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ تَدُورُ أَعْيُنُهُمْ كَٱلَّذِى يُغْشَىٰ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۖ فَإِذَا ذَهَبَ ٱلْخَوْفُ سَلَقُوكُم بِأَلْسِنَةٍ حِدَادٍ أَشِحَّةً عَلَى ٱلْخَيْرِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ فَأَحْبَطَ ٱللَّهُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا (١٩)
grudging you [their help]. So when there is panic, you see them looking at you, with their eyes rolling like someone fainting at death. Then, when the panic is over, they scald you with [their] sharp tongues in their greed for the spoils. They never have had faith. So Allah has made their works fail, and that is easy for Allah. (19)
يَحْسَبُونَ ٱلْأَحْزَابَ لَمْ يَذْهَبُوا۟ ۖ وَإِن يَأْتِ ٱلْأَحْزَابُ يَوَدُّوا۟ لَوْ أَنَّهُم بَادُونَ فِى ٱلْأَعْرَابِ يَسْـَٔلُونَ عَنْ أَنۢبَآئِكُمْ ۖ وَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ فِيكُم مَّا قَـٰتَلُوٓا۟ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٢٠)
They suppose the confederates have not left yet, and were the confederates to come [again], they would wish they were in the desert with the Bedouins asking about your news, and if they were with you they would fight but a little. (20)
لَّقَدْ كَانَ لَكُمْ فِى رَسُولِ ٱللَّهِ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ لِّمَن كَانَ يَرْجُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلْيَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَ وَذَكَرَ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا (٢١)
There is certainly a good exemplar for you in the Apostle of Allah—for those who look forward to Allah and the Last Day, and remember Allah much. (21)
وَلَمَّا رَءَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ٱلْأَحْزَابَ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا مَا وَعَدَنَا ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَصَدَقَ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ ۚ وَمَا زَادَهُمْ إِلَّآ إِيمَـٰنًا وَتَسْلِيمًا (٢٢)
But when the faithful saw the confederates, they said, ‘This is what Allah and His Apostle had promised us, and Allah and His Apostle were true.’ And it only increased them in faith and submission. (22)
مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ رِجَالٌ صَدَقُوا۟ مَا عَـٰهَدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَلَيْهِ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مَّن قَضَىٰ نَحْبَهُۥ وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَنتَظِرُ ۖ وَمَا بَدَّلُوا۟ تَبْدِيلًا (٢٣)
Among the faithful are men who fulfill what they have pledged to Allah: there are some among them who have fulfilled their pledge, and some of them who still wait, and they have not changed in the least, (23)
لِّيَجْزِىَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ بِصِدْقِهِمْ وَيُعَذِّبَ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ إِن شَآءَ أَوْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (٢٤)
that Allah may reward the true for their truthfulness, and punish the hypocrites, if He wishes, or accept their repentance. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (24)
وَرَدَّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِغَيْظِهِمْ لَمْ يَنَالُوا۟ خَيْرًا ۚ وَكَفَى ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱلْقِتَالَ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ قَوِيًّا عَزِيزًا (٢٥)
Allah sent back the faithless in their rage, without their attaining any advantage, and Allah spared the faithful of fighting, and Allah is all-strong, all-mighty. (25)
وَأَنزَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَـٰهَرُوهُم مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِن صَيَاصِيهِمْ وَقَذَفَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلرُّعْبَ فَرِيقًا تَقْتُلُونَ وَتَأْسِرُونَ فَرِيقًا (٢٦)
And He dragged down from their strongholds those who had backed them from among the People of the Book, and He cast terror into their hearts, [so that] you killed a part of them, and took captive [another] part. (26)
وَأَوْرَثَكُمْ أَرْضَهُمْ وَدِيَـٰرَهُمْ وَأَمْوَٰلَهُمْ وَأَرْضًا لَّمْ تَطَـُٔوهَا ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرًا (٢٧)
He bequeathed you their land, their houses and their possessions, and a territory you had not trodden, and Allah has power over all things. (27)

(Commentary)

The story of the Battle of the Trench (غزوة الخندق) and the subsequent affair of Banu Qurayzah, and the reason for its connection to what preceded it, lies in the mention of upholding covenants and the breaking thereof.

His saying, the Exalted: «O you who have faith! Remember Allah’s blessing upon you when hosts came at you» [33:9] etc., is a reminder to the believers of the favor He bestowed upon them during the days of the Trench (الخندق) by granting them victory and turning away the hosts of the polytheists from them. They were mobilized armies from various peoples and tribes, such as Ghatafan, Quraysh, the Abyssinians (الأحابيش), Kinanah, and the Jews of Banu Qurayzah and al-Nadir. They surrounded them from above them and from below them, so Allah unleashed the wind upon them and sent down angels to discourage and defeat them.

This is His saying: «O you who have faith! Remember Allah’s blessing upon you when»—the word "when" (إذ) is an adverbial qualifier for the blessing or its realization—«hosts came at you» from various factions, each of which was a host (جند), such as Ghatafan, Quraysh, and others. «So We sent» is an explanation of the blessing, which was the sending that followed their arrival, «a gale upon them»—which was the east wind (الصبا), and it was cold during wintry nights—«and hosts you did not see»—which were the angels sent to demoralize the polytheists—«and Allah sees best what you do.»

His saying, the Exalted: «When they came at you from above you and from below you» [33:10] etc. Those who came from above them—which was the eastern side of Medina—were Ghatafan and the Jews of Banu Qurayzah and Banu al-Nadir. Those who came from below them—which was the western side—were Quraysh and those who joined them from the Abyssinians and Kinanah. Thus, His saying: «When they came at you from above you and from below you» serves as an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان) for His saying: «when hosts came at you.»

And His saying: «when the eyes rolled, and the hearts reached the throats» is another explanatory apposition for His saying: «when hosts came at you» etc. The rolling of the eyes (زيغ الأبصار) refers to their deviating or staring wildly, and the hearts (القلوب) refer to the souls (الأنفس), and the throats (الحناجر) is the plural of hanjar (حنجر), which is the interior of the windpipe.

These two descriptions—namely, the rolling of the eyes and the hearts reaching the throats—are metaphors for the absolute pervasiveness of fear within them, to the point that it transformed them into the state of a dying person whose gaze wanders and whose soul reaches the throat.

And His saying: «and you entertained [evil] thoughts about Allah» means that the hypocrites and those in whose hearts was a sickness entertained various thoughts. Some said: "The disbelievers will surely triumph and seize Medina"; some said: "Islam will be eradicated and the religion lost"; some said: "The Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) will return as it was"; and some said: "Allah and His Messenger have deceived them," among other such thoughts.

His saying, the Exalted: «There the faithful were tested, and they were shaken with a violent concussion» [33:11]. There (هنالك) is a distal demonstrative pointing to a time or place; here it refers to the time the hosts arrived, which was extremely difficult for them.

Testing (الابتلاء) means trial (الامتحان), and shaking (الزلزلة والزلزال) means agitation (الاضطراب), and severity (الشدة) means strength (القوة). They differ in that "severity" is predominantly used for that which is perceived by the senses, unlike "strength." It is said that for this reason, the term "The Strong" (القوي) is applied to the Exalted, but not "The Severe" (الشديد).

The meaning is: In that difficult time, the believers were tested and were severely agitated out of fear.

His saying, the Exalted: «And when the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness would say, ‘Allah and His Apostle did not promise us [anything] but delusion’» [33:12]. Those in whose hearts is a sickness are the weak in faith among the believers; they are distinct from the hypocrites (المنافقون) who manifest faith while concealing disbelief. The hypocrites only mentioned "the Messenger" because of their outward display of Islam.

Delusion (الغرور) is to incite a person toward evil by presenting it in the form of good, and being deluded (الاغترار) is to be susceptible to it.

Al-Raghib said: It is said, "I deluded (غررت) so-and-so," meaning I caught him in a state of heedlessness (غرته) and obtained from him what I desired. And al-ghirrah (الغرة)—with a kasrah on the ghayn—is heedlessness while awake. (End of quote).

The promise they considered a delusion from Allah and His Messenger, given the context, was the promise of victory and the triumph of Islam over all religions. This promise was repeated in the speech of the Exalted, and it is narrated that the hypocrites said: "Muhammad promises us that we will conquer the cities of Khosrow and Caesar, yet we do not feel safe even going to the latrine."

His saying, the Exalted: «And when a group of them said, ‘O people of Yathrib! There is no abiding for you [here], so go back’» [33:13]. Yathrib was the name of Medina before Islam; then the name "City of the Messenger" (مدينة الرسول) became dominant after the Migration (الهجرة), and then simply "The City" (المدينة). Al-muqam (المقام)—with a dammah on the mim—means residing or staying. Their saying: «There is no abiding for you [here], so go back» means there is no reason for you to stay here facing the hosts of the polytheists, for victory will undoubtedly be theirs, so return [to your homes]. Then He followed this by recounting what others said, saying—conjoined to "a group said": «and a part of them»—meaning of the hypocrites and those in whose hearts was a sickness—«sought the Prophet’s leave» to return, «saying» as a pretext for seeking leave, «‘Our houses are exposed’»—meaning they have defects or are insecure (عورة), such that the owner does not feel safe from the entry of a thief or the advancement of the enemy—«though they were not exposed. They did not intend»—meaning they did not intend by this statement of theirs—«anything but to flee.»

His saying, the Exalted: «Had they been invaded from its flanks and had they been asked to apostatize, they would have done it, and they would have hesitated but little» [33:14]. The plural pronouns refer to the hypocrites and those with diseased hearts. The pronoun in «invaded» (دُخِلَتْ) refers to the houses. The meaning of "invaded from its flanks" is that if the enemy hosts entered the houses from their sides while they were inside, and then they were asked for "the trial" (الفتنة)—which in this context means apostasy (الردة) and turning back from the religion—and were asked to commit it, they would have granted what was requested of them and would not have delayed in their apostasy except for a short time, the duration of the request itself. That is, they remain upon the religion only as long as there is ease; but when hardship and might strike them, they do not hesitate to turn back.

His saying, the Exalted: «Certainly they had already pledged to Allah before that they would not turn their backs, and Allah’s pledge is [always] questioned» [33:15]. The lam is for an oath. His saying: «they would not turn their backs» means they would not flee from battle, which is an explanation of the pledge. Perhaps what is meant by their pledge "before" is their pledge of allegiance (بيعة) through faith in Allah, His Messenger, and what His Messenger brought—and among what he brought was Jihad (الجهاد), in which fleeing is forbidden. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: «Say, ‘Flight will not avail you if you flee from death or being killed, and then you will be let to enjoy but a little’» [33:16]. Since every soul must die at a decreed and inevitable term (أجل) that is neither delayed nor advanced by an hour, fleeing has no effect on delaying the term whatsoever.

And His saying: «and then you will be let to enjoy but a little» means: even if fleeing were to benefit you and you were granted enjoyment by the delay of your term—hypothetically—that enjoyment would only be a meager enjoyment or for a short time, because its end is inevitably cut off.

His saying, the Exalted: «Say, ‘Who is it that can protect you from Allah if He desires any ill for you, or desires to grant you a mercy?’ They will not find for themselves any guardian or helper besides Allah» [33:17]. The previous verse was a reminder to them that human life is a decreed term, against which fleeing from the battlefield does not avail. This verse is a reminder that evil and good are purely subject to the will of Allah; no cause among causes can prevent its execution, and no one can protect man from it. Thus, prudence lies in entrusting the matter to the will of the Exalted and remaining firm upon His command by relying (التوكل) upon Him.

Since their hearts were diseased or occupied with concealed disbelief, He turned from commanding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to address them directly, to addressing Himself, saying: «They will not find for themselves any guardian or helper besides Allah.»

His saying, the Exalted: «Allah already knows the hinderers among you—to His saying—easy» [33:18-19]. Hindering (التعويق) is to discourage and turn away. Halumma (هلم) is a verbal noun meaning "come hither"; it is not dualized or pluralized in the dialect of al-Hijaz. Al-ba's (البأس) is severity and war. Ashihhah (أشحة) is the plural of shahih (شحيح), meaning stingy or miserly. The one who "faints" (يغشى عليه) is the one overcome by a swoon (الغشوة) such that his senses fail and his eyes begin to roll. Al-salq (السلق)—with a fathah then a sukun—is to strike or pierce.

The meaning of the two verses is: Allah indeed knows those among you who discourage people and turn them away from fighting—and they are the hypocrites—and He knows those among the hypocrites who say to their brothers among the hypocrites or those weak in faith, "Come to us," while they themselves do not attend the war except rarely, being stingy toward you with their very lives.

So when fear comes due to the appearance of the signs of battle, you see them looking at you out of fear with a gaze in which they have no will and in which their eyes have no stability; their eyes roll like one fainting from death. But when the fear departs, they lash you and pierce you with sharp, cutting tongues, while being stingy regarding the good you have attained.

Those have not believed, and faith has not settled in their hearts, even if they manifested it with their tongues; so Allah has nullified their deeds and made them fail, and that was easy for Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: «They suppose the factions have not left» [33:20] to the end of the verse. That is, out of extreme fear, they think that the factions (الأحزاب)—who are the hosts of the polytheists allied against the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—have not yet departed. «And should the factions come» a second time after having left and abandoned Medina, «they would wish» and love «that they were in the desert» (بادون)—meaning outside Medina in the wilderness—«among the Bedouins, asking for news of you» and your reports. «And were they to be among you» and had not gone out to the desert, «they would not fight, but a little»—meaning there is no great benefit in their staying with you or being with you, for they would not fight except for a negligible amount.

His saying, the Exalted: «In the Apostle of Allah there is certainly for you a good exemplar, for those who look forward to Allah and the Last Day, and remember Allah much» [33:21]. The exemplar (الأسوة) is the model (القدوة), which is emulation and following. His saying: «in the Apostle of Allah» means in the case of the Messenger of Allah; the emulation that exists in his case is their taking him as a model and their following him. The expression «there is certainly for you» (لقد كان لكم), which indicates stability and continuity in the past, is an allusion to it being a fixed and continuous duty.

The meaning is: It is part of the requirement of the Messenger's message and your faith in him that you emulate him in his speech and action, as you see what he endures for the sake of Allah, his presence in battle, and his striving (جاهد) for Allah with the striving that is His due.

In al-Kashshaf: "If you ask: What is the reality of His saying: «In the Apostle of Allah there is certainly for you a good exemplar»? (And uswah is also read with a dammah). I say: There are two aspects: First, that he is in himself a good exemplar, i.e., a model to be emulated, just as you say: 'In the egg is twenty manns of iron,' meaning it is in itself that amount of iron. Second, that in him is a quality that deserves to be emulated and followed, which is his self-sacrifice (المواساة بنفسه)." (End of quote). The first of the two aspects is close to what we have presented.

And His saying: «for those who look forward to Allah and the Last Day, and remember Allah much» is a substitute (بدل) for the pronoun of address in «for you» (لكم), to indicate that emulating the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is a beautiful and pure trait that not everyone who is called a believer possesses. Rather, it is possessed by a group of those who have clothed themselves in the reality of faith, such that they look forward to Allah and the Last Day—meaning their hearts are attached to Allah, so they believe in Him, and their hearts are attached to the Last Day, so they perform righteous deeds—and along with that, they remember Allah much, never being heedless of their Lord; thus they emulate the Prophet in his acts and deeds.

It is also said that His saying: «for those who...» etc., is a relative clause for His saying: «good» (حسنة) or an attribute for it, to prevent it being a substitute for the pronoun of address. The outcome of the three views in terms of meaning is one.

His saying, the Exalted: «When the faithful saw the factions, they said, ‘This is what Allah and His Apostle had promised us, and Allah and His Apostle spoke the truth’» [33:22]. This is a description of the state of the believers when they witnessed the factions and the arrival of their armies around Medina. This was a cause for their maturity and insight in faith, and their confirmation of Allah and His Messenger, contrary to what appeared from the hypocrites and those in whose hearts was a sickness of doubt and evil speech. By this, it becomes clear that the "believers" intended here are those sincere in their faith in Allah and His Messenger.

His saying: «they said, ‘This is what Allah and His Apostle had promised us’»—the demonstrative "this" (هذا) refers to what they witnessed, stripped of other specificities, as in His saying: «When he saw the sun rising, he said, ‘This is my Lord’» [6:78].

Regarding the promise they pointed to, it is said: It is what the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had promised them—that the factions would ally against them; so when they saw them, it became clear to them that this was what he had promised.

It is also said: They had heard the saying of the Exalted in Surah al-Baqarah: «Do you suppose that you will enter paradise though there has not yet come to you the like of [what befell] those who went before you? Distress and misfortune settled upon them, and they were so shaken that the Apostle and the faithful who were with him said, ‘When will Allah’s help come?’ Look! Allah’s help is indeed near» [2:214]. Thus they realized that they would be afflicted by what afflicted the prophets and the believers before them of hardship and trial that shakes hearts and dazes souls. So when they saw the factions, they were certain it was part of the promised pledge and that Allah would grant them victory over their enemy.

The truth is to combine the two views, given that they joined Allah and His Messenger in the promise when they said: «This is what Allah and His Apostle had promised us.»

And His saying: «and Allah and His Apostle spoke the truth» is a testimony from them to the truthfulness of the promise. And His saying: «and it only increased them in faith and submission» means faith in Allah and His Messenger, and submission (تسليماً) to Allah's command to support His religion and strive in His way.

His saying, the Exalted: «Among the faithful are men who fulfill what they have pledged to Allah: of them are some who have fulfilled their pledge, and of them are some who still wait, and they have not changed in the least» [33:23]. Al-Raghib said: The pledge (النحب) is the vow (النذر) whose obligation is decreed. It is said, "So-and-so fulfilled his nahb," meaning he fulfilled his vow. The Exalted said: «of them are some who have fulfilled their pledge, and of them are some who still wait.» This is also used as an expression for one who has died, as in their saying: "He fulfilled his term, completed his sustenance, and fulfilled his need from the world." (End of quote).

His saying: «who fulfill what they have pledged to Allah» means they realized their truthfulness in what they had pledged to Him—that they would not flee when meeting the enemy. The evidence that this is the intended meaning of the pledge is that the verse parallels the previous statement regarding the hypocrites and those weak in faith: «Certainly they had already pledged to Allah before that they would not turn their backs.» Similarly, the previous verse parallels what was mentioned earlier regarding the people's doubt and their lack of submission to Allah's command.

His saying: «of them are some who have fulfilled their pledge» etc., means some of them have completed their term through death or being killed in the way of Allah, and some of them are waiting for that, and they have not changed anything of what they were upon—of word or pledge—in the least.

His saying, the Exalted: «That Allah may reward the truthful for their truthfulness, and punish the hypocrites, if He wishes, or accept their repentance. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful» [33:24]. The lam is for the ultimate purpose (الغاية), and what the verse contains is the purpose for all those mentioned previously, both hypocrites and believers.

Thus, His saying: «That Allah may reward the truthful for their truthfulness»—the "truthful" (الصادقين) refers to the believers, and their truthfulness was mentioned before. The ba in «for their truthfulness» (بصدقهم) is for causality, meaning: so that He may reward the believers who were true to their pledge because of their truthfulness.

And His saying: «and punish the hypocrites, if He wishes, or accept their repentance» means: and so that He may punish the hypocrites if He wills to punish them—and that is in the event they do not repent—or accept their repentance if they repent. Indeed, Allah is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful.

In the verse, insofar as it is an explanation of the ultimate purpose, there is a subtle point: that sins might sometimes be a prelude to felicity and forgiveness—not because they are sins, but because they drive the soul from darkness and wretchedness to a point where the soul becomes repulsed and awakened, thus repenting to its Lord and tearing itself away from its transgressions and sins; so Allah accepts its repentance in the end.

His saying, the Exalted: «Allah sent back the faithless in their rage, without their attaining any advantage, and Allah spared the faithful any fighting, and Allah is all-strong, all-mighty» [33:25]. Rage (الغيظ) is grief and resentment. What is meant by "advantage" (الخير) is what the disbelievers considered an advantage, which was triumphing over the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the believers.

The meaning is: Allah sent back those who disbelieved along with their grief and resentment, while they had not attained what they had hoped for. Allah spared the believers from fighting, so they did not fight, and Allah was Strong over what He wills, Mighty (عزيزاً) and never overcome.

His saying, the Exalted: «He brought down those of the People of the Book who had backed them from their fortresses—to His saying—all-powerful» [33:26-27]. Backing (المظاهرة) is assistance. Siyasi (صياصي) is the plural of sisiyah (صيصية), which is the fortress (الحصن) in which one takes refuge. Perhaps the expression "brought down" (الإنزال) is used instead of "brought out" because those taking refuge ascend the towers of the fortresses and look down from them and from the tops of the walls upon their enemies outside who are besieging them.

The meaning is: «He brought down those who had backed them»—meaning assisted the polytheists, and they were Banu Qurayzah—«from the People of the Book»—who were the Jews—«from their fortresses» and strongholds, «and He cast» and threw «terror into their hearts» and fear. «A group you killed»—and they were the men—«and a group you took captive»—and they were the children and women. «And He made you heirs to»—meaning He gave you possession after them of—«their land, their dwellings, and their possessions, and a land you had never trodden»—which was the land of Khaybar, or the land that Allah granted as spoils (أفاء) from that which was not attacked with horses or camels. As for the interpretation that it refers to every land that will be conquered until the Day of Resurrection, or the land of Mecca, or the land of Rome and Persia, the context of the two verses does not suit it. «And Allah has power over all things.»

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', Muhammad ibn Ka'b al-Qurazi and others among the biographers (أصحاب السير) mentioned, they said: Part of the account of the Trench (الخندق) was that a group of Jews—among them Sallam ibn Abi al-Huqayq and Huyayy ibn Akhtab among a group of Banu al-Nadir, whom the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had expelled—went out until they came to Quraysh in Mecca. They invited them to war against the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: "We will be with you against them until we eradicate them."

Quraysh said to them: "O assembly of Jews, you are the people of the first Scripture—is our religion better or the religion of Muhammad?" They said: "Rather, your religion is better than his religion, and you are more entitled to the truth than him." They are the ones concerning whom Allah revealed: «Have you not regarded those who were given a share of the Book? They believe in Jibt and Taghut and say of the faithless, ‘These are better guided on the way than those who have faith’—to His saying—and hell suffices as a blaze» [4:51-55]. Quraysh were pleased by what they said and became eager for what they were invited to; so they gathered for that and made an appointment for it.

Then that group of Jews went out until they came to Ghatafan and invited them to war against the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). They informed them that they would be against him and that Quraysh had pledged to them for that; so they responded to them.

Quraysh went out, and their leader was Abu Sufyan ibn Harb. Ghatafan went out, and their leader was 'Uyaynah ibn Hisn ibn Hudhayfah ibn Badr—among Fazara; and al-Harith ibn 'Awf among Banu Murrah; and Mis'ar ibn Jabalah al-Ashja'i among those who followed him from Ashja'. They wrote to their allies from Banu Asad, so Tulayhah approached among those who followed him from Banu Asad—and Asad and Ghatafan were allies. Quraysh wrote to men from Banu Sulaym, so Abu al-A'war al-Sulami approached among those who followed him from Banu Sulaym as reinforcements for Quraysh.

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) learned of that, he dug the trench around Medina. The one who suggested it was Salman al-Farisi, and it was the first battle Salman witnessed with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while he was a free man. He said: "O Messenger of Allah, in Persia, when we were besieged, we would dig a trench around us." So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the Muslims worked on it until they completed it.

Among the signs of prophethood (دلائل النبوة) that appeared during the digging of the trench is what Abu 'Abdillah al-Hafiz narrated with his chain of transmission from Kathir ibn 'Abdillah ibn 'Amr ibn 'Awf al-Muzani, who said: My father narrated to me from his father, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) marked out the trench in the Year of the Factions (الأحزاب), forty cubits between every ten men. The Emigrants (المهاجرون) and the Helpers (الأنصار) disagreed over Salman al-Farisi—and he was a strong man. The Helpers said: "Salman is from us," and the Emigrants said: "Salman is from us." So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: سلمان منا أهل البيت — "Salman is from us, the People of the House."

'Amr ibn 'Awf said: So I, Salman, Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman, al-Nu'man ibn Muqarrin, and six of the Helpers were cutting forty cubits. We dug until we reached the moist earth (الثرى). Allah brought out from the belly of the trench a white, round rock. It broke our iron tools and was difficult for us. We said: "O Salman, go up to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and inform him about the rock. Either we should divert from it—for the diversion is near—or he should command us regarding it, for we do not like to exceed his line." Salman went up until he came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while he was in a dome-tent (قبة). He said: "O Messenger of Allah, a white, round rock has emerged from the trench; it has broken our iron and is difficult for us, such that nothing can scratch it. So command us regarding it." The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) descended with Salman into the trench, took the pickaxe, and struck it once. A flash of lightning shone from it, illuminating what was between its two lava-fields (لابتيها)—meaning the two lava-fields of Medina—until it was like a lamp in the heart of a dark night. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) shouted "Allahu Akbar" (تكبيرة), a cry of victory, and the Muslims shouted "Allahu Akbar." Then he struck it a second time, and another flash shone. Then he struck it a third time, and another flash shone.

Salman said: "May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, O Messenger of Allah! What is this that I see?" He said: أما الأولى فإن الله عز وجل فتح علي بها اليمن ـ وأما الثانية فإن الله فتح علي بها الشام والمغرب وأما الثالثة فإن الله فتح علي بها المشرق — "As for the first, Allah, Mighty and Majestic, has opened Yemen for me through it. As for the second, Allah has opened the Levant (الشام) and the West for me through it. As for the third, Allah has opened the East for me through it." The Muslims rejoiced at that and said: "Praise be to Allah, a true promise." He said: And the factions appeared, so the believers said: «This is what Allah and His Apostle had promised us, and Allah and His Apostle spoke the truth.» But the hypocrites said: "Are you not amazed? He speaks to you and promises you falsehood; he tells you that he sees from Yathrib the palaces of al-Hirah and the cities of Khosrow, and that they will be opened for you, while you are digging the trench and cannot even go out [to relieve yourselves]."

Among the signs of prophethood that appeared therein is also what Abu 'Abdillah al-Hafiz narrated with his chain of transmission from 'Abd al-Wahid ibn Ayman al-Makhzumi, who said: Ayman al-Makhzumi narrated to me, saying: I heard Jabir ibn 'Abdillah say: On the day of the Trench, we were digging the trench when a hard mound (كدية)—which is a rock—appeared in it. We said: "O Messenger of Allah, a hard mound has appeared in it." The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: رشوا عليها ماء — "Sprinkle water on it." Then he stood and came to it, while his stomach was bound with a stone (حجر) from hunger. He took the pickaxe or the shovel, mentioned the name of Allah thrice, then struck, and it turned into a heap (كثيباً) of pouring sand. I said: "Permit me, O Messenger of Allah, to go home." He did so. I said to my wife: "Do you have anything?" She said: "I have a sa' of barley and a young she-goat (عناق)." I ground the barley and kneaded it, slaughtered the young goat and skinned it, and left the woman to attend to that.

Then I came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and sat with him for a while. Then I said: "Permit me, O Messenger of Allah." He did so. I came to the woman and found the dough and the meat were ready. I returned to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: "We have a little food (طعيماً) for us; so come, O Messenger of Allah, you and two of your companions." He said: "How much is it?" I said: "A sa' of barley and a young goat." He said to all the Muslims: قوموا إلى جابر — "Rise and go to Jabir." They rose, and I felt such shame as only Allah knows. I said: "He has brought the whole creation to a sa' of barley and a young goat!"

I entered upon the woman and said: "I have been disgraced! The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) has come to you with all the people." She said: "Did he ask you how much your food was?" I said: "Yes." She said: "Allah and His Messenger know best; we have informed him of what we have." This removed a great distress from me.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) entered and said: خذي ودعيني من اللحم — "Take [the dough] and leave the meat to me." The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) began to crumble the bread and distribute the meat. He would cover this [pot] and cover that [oven]. He continued to serve the people until they were all full, yet the oven and the pot remained as full as they were.

Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: كلي وأهدي — "Eat and give as gifts." So we continued to eat and give to all our people. Al-Bukhari recorded it in the Sahih.

They said: When the Messenger of Allah finished the trench, Quraysh approached until they camped between al-Jurf and al-Ghabah with ten thousand of their Abyssinians and those who followed them from Banu Kinanah and the people of Tihamah. Ghatafan and those who followed them from the people of Najd approached until they camped beside Uhud. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the Muslims went out until they placed their backs toward Sal'—among three thousand Muslims. He set up his camp there with the trench between him and the people. He ordered the children and women to be placed in the fortified towers (الآطام).

The enemy of Allah, Huyayy ibn Akhtab al-Nadiri, went out until he came to Ka'b ibn Asad al-Qurazi, the leader of Banu Qurayzah. He had made a peace treaty with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) on behalf of his people and had pledged to him for that. When Ka'b heard the voice of Ibn Akhtab, he closed his fortress against him. He sought permission to enter, but he refused to open for him. He called out: "O Ka'b, open for me!" He said: "Woe to you, O Huyayy! You are an ill-omened man. I have a covenant with Muhammad and I am not one to break what is between me and him; I have seen nothing from him but fulfillment and truthfulness." He said: "Woe to you! Open for me so I may speak to you." He said: "I will not do so." He said: "You have only closed [the door] against me because of your gruel (جشيشة), fearing that I might eat from it with you."

This provoked the man, so he opened for him. He said: "Woe to you, O Ka'b! I have brought you the glory of the age and a surging sea (بحر طام). I have brought you Quraysh with their leaders and nobles, and Ghatafan with their nobles and leaders. They have pledged to me that they will not leave until they eradicate Muhammad and those with him." Ka'b said: "By Allah, you have brought me the humiliation of the age and a waterless cloud (جهام) that has poured its water, thundering and lightning but containing nothing. Leave me and Muhammad and what I am upon, for I have seen nothing from Muhammad but truthfulness and fulfillment."

Huyayy continued to manipulate Ka'b—"twisting him by the hump and the withers" (يفتل منه في الذروة والغارب)—until he yielded to him, on the condition that he gave him a covenant and a solemn pledge: "If Quraysh and Ghatafan return without having harmed Muhammad, I will enter your fortress with you until what befalls you befalls me." So Ka'b broke his covenant and disavowed what he was upon between him and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

When the news reached the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), he sent Sa'd ibn Mu'adh ibn al-Nu'man ibn Imri' al-Qays—one of the Banu 'Abd al-Ashhal and the leader of al-Aws at that time—and Sa'd ibn 'Ubadah—one of the Banu Sa'idah ibn Ka'b ibn al-Khazraj and the leader of al-Khazraj at that time—along with 'Abdullah ibn Rawahah and Khawwat ibn Jubayr. He said: انطلقوا ـ حتى تنظروا أحق ما بلغنا عن هؤلاء القوم أم لا؟ فإن كان حقا فالحنوا لنا لحنا نعرفه ـ ولا تفتوا أعضاد الناس وإن كانوا على الوفاء فاجهروا به للناس — "Go until you see if what has reached us about these people is true or not. If it is true, then speak to us in a coded way (فالحنوا لنا لحنا) that we recognize, and do not break the strength of the people. But if they are faithful, then announce it openly to the people."

They went out until they reached them and found them in a worse state than what had reached them. They said: "There is no contract between us and Muhammad, nor any covenant." Sa'd ibn 'Ubadah traded insults with them, and they with him. Sa'd ibn Mu'adh said: "Leave off insulting them, for what is between us and them is greater than insulting."

Then they returned to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: "Adal and al-Qarah"—referring to the treachery of Adal and al-Qarah against the companions of the Messenger of Allah, Khubayb ibn 'Adi and his companions, the people of al-Raji'. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: الله أكبر ، أبشروا يا معشر المسلمين — "Allah is the Greatest! Rejoice, O assembly of Muslims!" At that, the affliction became great and fear intensified. Their enemy came at them from above them and from below them, until the believers entertained every kind of thought and hypocrisy appeared from some of the hypocrites.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) remained, and the polytheists remained against him for twenty-odd nights. There was no fighting between them except for the shooting of arrows, except that some horsemen of Quraysh—among them 'Amr ibn 'Abd Wudd, the brother of Banu 'Amir ibn Lu'ayy; 'Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl; Dirar ibn al-Khattab; Hubayrah ibn Abi Wahb; and Nawfal ibn 'Abdullah—had prepared for battle. They went out on their horses until they passed by the dwellings of Banu Kinanah and said: "Prepare for war, O Banu Kinanah! Today you shall know who the horsemen are."

Then they approached, their horses galloping (تعنق) with them, until they stood at the trench. They said: "By Allah, this is a stratagem that the Arabs have never used." Then they sought a narrow place in the trench, struck their horses, and leaped across. They galloped in the salt-marsh (السبخة) between the trench and Sal'. 'Ali ibn Abi Talib went out with a group of Muslims until he blocked the breach from which they had leaped, and the horsemen approached them.
